coredata.dc:title: Phenothiazine-based porous organic polymers with high sensitivity and selective fluorescence response to mercury ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hongyu
Qiu, Na
Kong, Xiangfei
Hu, Zhenguang
Zhong, Fuxin
Tan, Haijun
coredata.dc:description: A new Porous Organic Polymer (POP), termed CTF-PTZ, was synthesized through polymerization of 10-methyl-10H-phenothiazine-3,7-dicarbonitrile in the presence of trifluoromethanesulfonic acid catalyst. Owing to the richness of heteroatoms such as N and S atoms in its molecular structure, the fluorescence response of CTF-PTZ to metal ions was studied, and it was found that CTF-PTZ had a specific fluorescence response to Hg2+. Thus, high selectivity in fluorescence responsiveness to Hg2+ of CTF-PTZ was revealed and the detection limit of it was up to be 2.09 ​× ​10−7 moL·L−1, which was found by fluorescence spectroscopy titration and fluorescence competition experiments. We successfully demonstrated that the fluorescence responsive POP material provides the promising potential for Hg2+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mercury ion detection
Phenothiazine
Fluorescence response
Porous organic polymer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Wide angle X-ray diffractions of CTF-PTZ.
FT-IR spectra for CTF-PTZ and starting materials.
N2 adsorption (Square symbols)/desorption (Circular symbols) isotherms at 77
K of CTF-PTZ. Inserts: DFT pore size distribution.
Solid-state fluorescence spectra of CTF-PTZ and fluorescence of CTF-PTZ in dioxane solution.
The fluorescence photographs of CTF-PTZ dispersed in different solvents, where the fluorescence is excited under 365 ​nm using a portable UV lamp.

Fluorescence spectra of CTF-PTZ in various solvents (0.5 ​mg ​mL−1,
λ e x
​= ​375 ​nm).

Fluorescence spectral responses of the CTF-PTZ in DOX solution upon addition of Ag+、Al3+、Ca2+、Cd2+、Co2+、Cr2+、Cu2+、Fe2+、Fe3+、Hg2+、K+、Mg2+、Na+、Ni2+、Pb2+、Zn2+ (
λ e x
​= ​375 ​nm).
Competitive selectivity of CTF-PTZ toward Hg2+ ion in the presence of other metal ions under an excitation of 375 ​nm.
Fluorescence spectra of the CTF-PTZ in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ ions.
Linear fitting curve of the CTF-PTZ with increasing concentrations of Hg2+ ions.
LODs of CTF-PTZ with other materials.

head.ce:title: Phenothiazine-based porous organic polymers with high sensitivity and selective fluorescence response to mercury ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004;

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new Porous Organic Polymer (POP), termed CTF-PTZ, was synthesized through polymerization of 10-methyl-10H-phenothiazine-3,7-dicarbonitrile in the presence of trifluoromethanesulfonic acid catalyst. Owing to the richness of heteroatoms such as N and S atoms in its molecular structure, the fluorescence response of CTF-PTZ to metal ions was studied, and it was found that CTF-PTZ had a specific fluorescence response to Hg2+. Thus, high selectivity in fluorescence responsiveness to Hg2+ of CTF-PTZ was revealed and the detection limit of it was up to be 2.09 ​× ​10−7 moL·L−1, which was found by fluorescence spectroscopy titration and fluorescence competition experiments. We successfully demonstrated that the fluorescence responsive POP material provides the promising potential for Hg2+ detection.

The TOC figure means that the fluorescence intensity of the CTF-PTZ in DOX solution decreases with the increase of mercury ion concentration, and can be directly observed with naked eye. To visually display the internal structure of CTF-PTZ, we also include its molecular structure. After that, the detection limit of mercury ions was calculated to be 2.09 ​× ​10−7 ​mol ​L−1 ​by titration experiment.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of heavy metal industries, the widespread contamination of heavy metal ions in the environment has been regarded as a great threat to human health. Mercury (Hg) is one of the most toxic heavy metals in the environment, which mainly exists in the form of metallic mercury, inorganic mercury and organic mercury. Among them, organic mercury is the most toxic, and mercury-polluted water bodies are extremely harmful to humans and aquatic organisms [1]. Due to its characteristics of high toxicity, non-degradability and bioaccumulation, the selective adsorption of Hg2+ from other types of heavy metal solutions is a critical issue to be solved. A variety of fluorescent sensors for Hg2+ detection have been reported [2–8], most of them are based on small-molecule compounds [9–12]. In recent years, polymer-based fluorescent sensors have received increasing attention due to their superiority in controllable pore structure [13,14] and good stability [15,16]. Ding et al. reported the first application of fluorescent COFs (COF-LZU8) for detecting and removing the Hg2+ with the detection limit of 25 ​ppb [17]. Wang et al. developed a sulfur-containing fluorescent porous polymer called HPP-SH which can detect and adsorb Hg2+ at the same time, and its detection limit reaches 4.48 ​ppb [18]. Li et al. designed a fluorescent porous organic polymer (TPA-POP-TSC), with a “fluorophore” backbone and a thiosemicarbazide “receptor” for Hg2+-targeted sensing, which possesses huge binding energy (−29.84 ​kJ ​mol−1) and extremely low detection limit (3.7 ​ppb) [19]. Fan et al. cut the low-cost layered covalent triazine-based frameworks (CTF-1) [20] into N-doped quantum dots (CTFQDs) which could be further utilized as an efficient PL probe for detecting Hg2+ with the detection limit of 0.02 ​ppb [21]. Among all kinds of polymers, triazine-based polymers have been widely noticed for their enrichment in nitrogen atoms, rigid structures and polar surfaces [22–25]. The specific functional groups can also be introduced to the main or branched-chain of the polymers to adapt to the electronic properties of the substances to be measured. It has great practical application prospects in the fields of the photoelectric effect [26], gas adsorption [27], separation [28], analysis [29,30], and chemical sensing [31].

Phenothiazine has the advantages of good electron-donating property, hole-transport property, easy structural modification and richness in N and S atoms [32,33]. It is widely used in photoelectric [34], fluorescence [35–40] and catalytic material molecules. Therefore, based on the above advantages, a new POP (CTF-PTZ) was synthesized by 10-methyl-10H-phenothiazine-3,7-dicarbonitrile polymerization in the presence of trifluoromethanesulfonic acid catalyst (Fig. 1 ). The fluorescence properties of CTF-PTZ are analyzed and applied to the specific fluorescence detection to metal ions.

Acetonitrile, acetone, dimethylformamide, 1,4-dioxane, trichloromethane, tetrahydrofuran, ethanol and trifluoromethanesulfonic acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+,Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Ca(NO3)2·9H2O, CdSO4·
8 3
H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Fe(NO3)2·9H2O, HgCl2, KCl, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, NaCl, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, PbSO4, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, NaH, CH3I, N-Bromosuccinimide, copper cyanide, 18-crown-6 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.

As demonstrated in Scheme 1 , the synthesis of CTF-PTZ is mainly based on the methylation, cyanidation and triazine cyclization of phenothiazine units. The synthetic route is simple, and the conditions are mild and economical. The specific synthesis process and detailed characterization data are as follows:

Synthesis of compound 2. Sodium hydride (2 ​g, 50 ​mmol) was added to a solution containing dry THF (150 ​ml) and phenothiazine (5 ​g, 25 ​mmol). After the solution was stirred at room temperature for 2 ​h, methyl iodide (6.14 ​ml, 100 ​mmol) was slowly added to the mixture and stirred at room temperature for another 2 ​h. Then pour the reaction solution into the water in batches, extracted with CH2Cl2 several times and collect the organic phase. After removing the solvent, the residue was purified by flash column chromatography with petroleum ether and dichloromethane (20:1, V/V) to afford the compound 2 as a brown solid (4.7933 ​g, yielding 89.6%). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 500 ​MHz), δ (TMS, ppm): 7.24-7.15 (m, 4H), 7.01-6.93 (t, 2H), 6.88-6.82 (d, 2H), 3.43-3.39 (s, 1H).

Synthesis of compound 3. Compound 2 (1.3958 ​g, 6.5 ​mmol) and N-bromosuccinimide (NBS) (2.55 ​g, 14.3 ​mmol) were dissolved in a double neck flask containing tetrahydrofuran (45 ​ml) in the dark. After stirring at room temperature for 3 ​h, the solvent was removed by rotary evaporation, extracted with CH2Cl2, and washed with water. The organic layers were combined, dried with anhydrous Na2SO4 and filtered. A crude product was obtained after removing all the solvent by rotary evaporation, and further purified by flash column chromatography with petroleum ether and dichloromethane (10:1, V/V) to afford the compound 3 as a white solid (2.06 ​g, yielding 85.1%). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 500 ​MHz) δ (TMS, ppm): 7.31-7.28 (d, 1H), 7.28-7.27 (d, 1H), 7.26-7.24 (d, 2H), 3.36-3.30 (s, 3H).

Synthesis of compound 4. Compound 3 (1.12 ​g, 3.0 ​mmol), CuCN (1.34 ​g, 15.0 ​mmol) and 18-crown-6 (0.1 ​g) were dissolved in dry DMF (30 ​ml) under Ar2 atmosphere. After the solution was stirred at room temperature for 48 ​h, the mixture was poured into ammonia (ammonia/distilled water ​= ​1/1 (V/V)) and stirred at room temperature for another 1 ​h. The precipitate was collected by filtration and washed with water and ethanol. Finally, the residue was purified by flash column chromatography with petroleum ether and dichloromethane (1:1, V/V) to afford the compound 4 as a bright green solid (0.3456 ​g, yielding 43.6%). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 500 ​MHz) δ (TMS, ppm): 7.537.47 (m, 2H), 7.40-7.35 (d, 2H), 6.90-6.85 (d, 2H), 3.48- 3.43 (s, 3H). 13C NMR (DMSO-d6, 400 ​MHz) δ (TMS, ppm):147.9, 132.3, 130.2, 123.6, 118.1, 114.6, 106.8, 35.9.

Synthesis of CTF-PTZ. The trifluoromethanesulfonic acid (TFMS) (0.87 ​ml, 10.9 ​mmol) in 5 ​mL of CHCl3 was charged into a two-neck flask under Ar2 atmosphere. The mixture was cooled to 0 ​°C, then add the chloroform solution (20 ​ml) of compound 4 (0.577 ​g, 2.2 ​mmol) into the solution dropwise over 30 ​min. The mixture was stirred at 0 ​°C for 2 ​h and stirred at room temperature for 48 ​h. The solution turned dark red and a solid precipitate was formed. Then, the mixture was poured into 200 ​mL of water containing 10 ​mL of ammonia solution and stirred for 2 ​h. The precipitates were filtered and washed with water, ethanol, acetone, and chloroform, then were extracted by Soxhlet with ethanol, chloroform, and acetone for 48 ​h, respectively. Finally, the solid was dried at vacuum at 70 ​°C for 24 ​h to get the CTF-PTZ with the yield of 65%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Structure, morphology, thermal stability and BET

ce:section.ce:para: Considering that CTF-PTZ contains a large π conjugation and more N and S coordination sites on the conjugated framework, the fluorescence sensing detection of metal ions by CTF-PTZ was investigated. After adding the same concentration of different metal ions (1 ​× ​10−4 ​M, Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+), the fluorescence comparison test revealed that the addition of Hg2+ had the most pronounced fluorescence quenching effect on the DOX solution of CTF-PTZ compared to other metal ions (Fig. 11 ). This simple and easy way to visually identify Hg2+ is attractive for any in-field applications.

Good selectivity is the important index to evaluate the practicability of the CTF-PTZ. Therefore, Hg2+ were mixed with other metal ions (concentration 5 ​× ​10−4 moL·L−1, Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+) in a 1:1 ratio into the DOX solution of CTF-PTZ. The fluorescence response of the solution to Hg2+ in mixed components and a single component was compared and analyzed. As shown in Fig. 12 , it is an indisputable fact that CTF-PTZ has good fluorescence quenching selectivity for Hg2+ which is better than that of other metal ions.

Furthermore, to further understand the sensitivity of CTF-PTZ in the detection of Hg2+, a series of concentrations of Hg2+ solutions and suspensions of CTF-PTZ in dioxane were prepared to study fluorescence spectroscopy titration experiments. As shown in Fig. 13 , the fluorescence intensity decreases with increasing Hg2+ concentration, which means that the fluorescence will be completely quenched when the concentration increases to a certain level. In addition, there is a good linear relationship between Hg2+ concentration and fluorescence intensity in a certain range. As shown in Fig. 14 , the fitted linear equation was obtained as
y = − 432.7 x
+
2598.8 ,
R 2
=
0.90022
.

The minimum detection limit can be calculated according to equation ① [42,43].
①
L O D =
3 δ
S
where LOD (mol·L−1): limit of detection. δ (mol·L−1): standard deviation of 10 blank.
measurements. S ​= ​432.7.

The slope of the linear fitting curve in the fluorescence spectrum titration experiment：
②
δ =
∑
t = 1
N
（
F t
−
F ¯
） ²
N − 1

The lowest detection limit is calculated as 2.09 ​× ​10−7 ​mol ​L−1, this value is comparable to or lower than those of previously reported Hg2+ sensors, such as hypercrosslinked Conjugated Microporous Polymer (HCMP-1,5 ​× ​10−8 ​M) [44], FCDs (2 ​× ​10−8 ​M) [45], sulfur-containing fluorescent hybrid porous polymer (HPP-SH, 8.4 ​× ​10−9 ​M) [46], but higher than those of some sensors such as nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs, 2.5 ​× ​10−6 ​M) [47], nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots(N, S/C-dot, 2 ​× ​10−6 ​M) [48], VAC (2.5 ​× ​10−6 ​M) [49] (Table 1 ).

In addition, CTF-PTZ has the best quenching effect on Hg2+ among so many metal ions, which may be related to a variety of factors. We speculate that the main reason for fluorescence quenching is the complexation of Hg (II) with S atoms (phenothiazine) and N atoms (triazine ring) [50]. Because Hg (II) is a soft acid and lack of ligand field stabilization energy, the use of soft donor atoms such as nitrogen and sulfur in the chelating unit generally increases its affinity and selectivity for Hg (II). Therefore, CTF-PTZ can be used for specific detection of Hg2+.

We successfully developed a new phenothiazine-based organic porous polymer, CTF-PTZ. Benefitting from the presence of N, S atoms and π-electrons in CTF-PTZ, it has high sensitivity and excellent selective fluorescence quenching response to metal ions, especially Hg2+, and its detection limit for Hg2+ reaches 2.09 ​× ​10−7 ​mol ​L−1. The advantages of CTF-PTZ shows its potential applications in the development of highly efficient detection of trace Hg2+ in the environment.

Hongyu Wang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiangfei Kong: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Fuxin Zhong: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Haijun Tan: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All authors acknowledge the financial support of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 21663010), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi, China (2015GXNSFBA139029, 2018GXNSFAA281115).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: McNutt, M., Mercury and Health. 2013, 341, 1430-1430.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescence turn-on sensor for Hg2+ with a simple receptor available in sulphide-rich environments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Fan, X. Peng, S. Wang, X. Liu, H. Li, S. Sun, A fluorescence turn-on sensor for Hg2+ with a simple receptor available in sulphide-rich environments, J Fluoresc. 2012, 22, 945-951..

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid polymer and metallogel networks based on a fluorene derivative as fluorescent and colourimetric chemosensors for Hg(II)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Pablos, J. L.; Ibeas, S.; Munoz, A.; Serna, F.; Garcia, F. C.; Garcia, J. M., Solid polymer and metallogel networks based on a fluorene derivative as fluorescent and colourimetric chemosensors for Hg(II). Reactive & Functional Polymers. 2014, 79, 14-23.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thioether-based fluorescent covalent organic framework for selective detection and facile removal of mercury(II)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ding, S. Y.; Dong, M.; Wang, Y. W.; Chen, Y. T.; Wang, H. Z.; Su, C. Y.; Wang, W., Thioether-Based Fluorescent Covalent Organic Framework for Selective Detection and Facile Removal of Mercury(II). J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2016, 138, 3031-3037.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (II) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, L. B.; Yu, B.; You, T. Y., Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (II) ions. Biosens. Bioelectron. 2015, 74, 263-269.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbazolic porous framework with tetrahedral core for gas uptake and tandem detection of iodide and mercury

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.-Q. Dang, H.-J. Wan, X.-M. Zhang, Carbazolic Porous Framework with Tetrahedral Core for Gas Uptake and Tandem Detection of Iodide and Mercury. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 2017, 21438-21446.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New fluorescent chemosensors for metal ions in solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Formica, M.; Fusi, V.; Giorgi, L.; Micheloni, M., New fluorescent chemosensors for metal ions in solution. Coord. Chem. Rev. 2012, 256, 170-192.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembly of alternating copolymer vesicles for the highly selective, sensitive and visual detection and quantification of aqueous Hg2+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, S. Y.; Li, Z.; Li, K.; Yu, X. Q., Small molecular fluorescent probes for the detection of lead, cadmium and mercury ions. Coord. Chem. Rev. 2021, 429, 20.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, X. Y.; Yan, B., Eu(III)-Functionalized MIL-124 as Fluorescent Probe for Highly Selectively Sensing Ions and Organic Small Molecules Especially for Fe(III) and Fe(II). ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 2015, 7, 721-729.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Carter, K. P.; Young, A. M.; Palmer, A. E., Fluorescent Sensors for Measuring Metal Ions in Living Systems. Chem. Rev. 2014, 114, 4564-4601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A selective turn-on fluorescent sensor for Hg (II) in living cells and tissues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, Y.; Shen, R.; Wang, Y. Z.; Qiu, F. Z.; Feng, Y.; Tang, X. L.; Bai, D. C.; Zhang, G. L.; Liu, W. S., A selective turn-on fluorescent sensor for Hg (II) in living cells and tissues. Sens. Actuator B-Chem. 2018, 255, 3479-3487.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controllable and rapid synthesis of conjugated microporous polymer membranes via interfacial polymerization for ultrafast molecular separation

J., Structural Evolution of 2D Microporous Covalent Triazine-Based Framework toward the Study of High-Performance Supercapacitors. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2015, 137, 219-225.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Rozyyev, V.; Yavuz, M. S.; Thirion, D.; Nguyen, T. S.; Nguyen, T. P. N.; Emwas, A. H.; Yavuz, C.
